SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear empirically supported: the S3G algorithm (max-entropy IRL style reward fitting with importance sampling + policy optimization via MDGPS) is evaluated on multiple MuJoCo tasks and shown to outperform the labeled-only and supervised reward-regression baselines and sometimes the oracle. The mathematical approach is plausible and consistent with prior max-ent IRL formulations, and the experimental methodology uses reasonable baselines and reports sample complexity; however the summary does not cite formal proofs or statistical significance testing. There are acknowledged limitations (assumption that labeled and unlabeled MDPs share a distribution and high-variance importance sampling early in training), which are relevant caveats but do not invalidate the main empirical claims. | B(4): The main claims are well supported by experiments on multiple MuJoCo tasks showing S3G outperforms labeled-only and supervised reward-regression baselines and occasionally the oracle, and the method and sample complexity are reported and code is available. Mathematical framing (maximum-entropy trajectory likelihood with importance sampling and entropy-regularized policy optimization) is plausible and internally consistent given the summary, and the paper acknowledges key limitations (shared MDP distribution assumption and high-variance importance sampling). Minor concerns remain about potential variance in the importance-sampling estimator early in training and no mention of statistical tests, but these do not appear to undermine the core technical claims.

The mathematical approach is plausible and consistent with prior max-ent IRL formulations, and the experimental methodology uses reasonable baselines and reports sample complexity; however the summary does not cite formal proofs or statistical significance testing. There are acknowledged limitations (assumption that labeled and unlabeled MDPs share a distribution and high-variance importance sampling early in training), which are relevant caveats but do not invalidate the main empirical claims.

Mathematical framing (maximum-entropy trajectory likelihood with importance sampling and entropy-regularized policy optimization) is plausible and internally consistent given the summary, and the paper acknowledges key limitations (shared MDP distribution assumption and high-variance importance sampling). Minor concerns remain about potential variance in the importance-sampling estimator early in training and no mention of statistical tests, but these do not appear to undermine the core technical claims.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from motivation to method to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (the S3G algorithm and its IRL-style reward inference) are stated early. The methodology and notation are presented clearly and consistently through a stepwise description of training and reward fitting, and experimental evaluation is informative (success-rate metrics and sample-complexity results reported, with details in Appendix B). Figures/tables and examples appear to support the claims, and writing quality is good with thorough related-work coverage; only minor presentation issues remain (some experimental details relegated to the appendix). | B(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow from problem statement to method and experiments, with the main contributions clearly stated early (proposing S3G and its IRL-style reward inference). Notation and methodology are presented coherently (the iterative (a)-(c) procedure is clearly described) and experiments, baselines, and evaluation metrics are clearly laid out for the MuJoCo tasks. Writing quality is noted as good and related work coverage thorough, though there are minor presentation issues (e.g., potential high-variance importance sampling is discussed as a limitation) that keep it from an exceptional rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from motivation to method to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (the S3G algorithm and its IRL-style reward inference) are stated early. The methodology and notation are presented clearly and consistently through a stepwise description of training and reward fitting, and experimental evaluation is informative (success-rate metrics and sample-complexity results reported, with details in Appendix B). Figures/tables and examples appear to support the claims, and writing quality is good with thorough related-work coverage; only minor presentation issues remain (some experimental details relegated to the appendix).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow from problem statement to method and experiments, with the main contributions clearly stated early (proposing S3G and its IRL-style reward inference). Notation and methodology are presented coherently (the iterative (a)-(c) procedure is clearly described) and experiments, baselines, and evaluation metrics are clearly laid out for the MuJoCo tasks. Writing quality is noted as good and related work coverage thorough, though there are minor presentation issues (e.g., potential high-variance importance sampling is discussed as a limitation) that keep it from an exceptional rating.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears to introduce a notable and practically focused twist on existing ideas by framing semi-supervised RL and proposing S3G as (per the authors) the first practical algorithm to generalize skills from a small labeled set to many unlabeled MDPs. Its core technique—using an inverse-RL–style procedure to learn a reward from rollouts of a policy trained in labeled settings and then optimizing in unlabeled settings with importance-sampled maximum-entropy likelihood—is a non-trivial combination and extension of IRL/maximum-entropy RL and guided cost learning/MDGPS. The empirical finding that learned reward shaping can even outperform an oracle further differentiates it from straightforward baselines, so the contribution is notably novel rather than merely a simple recombination of known pieces. | B(4): The paper presents a clearly new, practical formulation and algorithm for semi-supervised reinforcement learning (S3G), claiming to be the first to leverage unlabeled MDPs by inferring a reward via an inverse-RL–style likelihood objective and then optimizing policies in unlabeled settings. This approach is meaningfully different from prior supervised reward regression baselines and standard RL (πRL), and the authors demonstrate empirical gains (even occasionally surpassing an oracle) on MuJoCo tasks. While the method builds on existing components (guided cost learning / MDGPS and maximum-entropy trajectory models), combining them into a general S3G pipeline that transfers from labeled to unlabeled MDPs is a non-trivial and notable extension that would be informative to experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears to introduce a notable and practically focused twist on existing ideas by framing semi-supervised RL and proposing S3G as (per the authors) the first practical algorithm to generalize skills from a small labeled set to many unlabeled MDPs. Its core technique—using an inverse-RL–style procedure to learn a reward from rollouts of a policy trained in labeled settings and then optimizing in unlabeled settings with importance-sampled maximum-entropy likelihood—is a non-trivial combination and extension of IRL/maximum-entropy RL and guided cost learning/MDGPS. The empirical finding that learned reward shaping can even outperform an oracle further differentiates it from straightforward baselines, so the contribution is notably novel rather than merely a simple recombination of known pieces.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a clearly new, practical formulation and algorithm for semi-supervised reinforcement learning (S3G), claiming to be the first to leverage unlabeled MDPs by inferring a reward via an inverse-RL–style likelihood objective and then optimizing policies in unlabeled settings. This approach is meaningfully different from prior supervised reward regression baselines and standard RL (πRL), and the authors demonstrate empirical gains (even occasionally surpassing an oracle) on MuJoCo tasks. While the method builds on existing components (guided cost learning / MDGPS and maximum-entropy trajectory models), combining them into a general S3G pipeline that transfers from labeled to unlabeled MDPs is a non-trivial and notable extension that would be informative to experts.

main_claims: Proposes the first practical algorithm for semi-supervised reinforcement learning (S3G) to generalize skills from a small set of labeled MDPs to a larger set of unlabeled MDPs.

Leverages unlabeled experience by using an inverse reinforcement learning–style procedure that learns a reward function from rollouts of a policy trained in labeled settings, and then optimizes a policy in unlabeled settings using that inferred reward.

Demonstrates in simulated MuJoCo tasks that S3G outperforms a policy trained only on labeled MDPs and a policy trained using supervised reward regression, and in some cases even outperforms an oracle due to learned reward shaping.
Method is compatible with any choice of RL/IRL algorithms; the experiments use guided cost learning / MDGPS and show sample-efficient training of neural network policies for continuous control tasks.
_summary.methodology: Train an entropy-regularized policy πRL in labeled MDPs and collect its rollouts DπRL; then iteratively (a) collect rollouts Dπθ from the current policy in unlabeled MDPs, (b) fit a parametric reward R̃φ by maximizing the likelihood of DπRL under a maximum-entropy trajectory distribution using importance sampling (with samples from DπRL and Dπθ), and (c) update the policy πθ in unlabeled MDPs to maximize expected R̃φ with an entropy term (implemented with MDGPS).
datasets: MuJoCo simulated tasks: obstacle navigation (obstacle height variant)
baselines: RL policy trained only in labeled MDPs (πRL)
Policy learned using supervised reward regression
Oracle policy with access to true reward in all scenarios
metrics: Success rate (task-specific success criteria, e.g., reaching within 5 cm or clearing wall)
Sample complexity (number of samples reported in Appendix B)
limitations: Assumes labeled and unlabeled MDPs are sampled from the same underlying distribution (no large, systematic domain shift).
Importance sampling estimate of the partition function Z can have high variance at the start of training when few policy samples have been collected.
